covered_sections: 3.1 Measures of Absolute Dispersions>3.1.1 Range and Inter-quartile Range
3.1 Measures of Absolute Dispersions>3.1.2 Mean Deviation and Quartile Deviation
3.1 Measures of Absolute Dispersions>3.1.3 Variance and Standard Deviation

mini_headings_covered: Mean deviation for discrete frequency distributions

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the range of the ungrouped data set \( \{12, 7, 9, 15, 20, 5\} \)?
questions.hint: Range is the difference between the largest and smallest values.
questions.explanation: Range is defined as the difference between the largest and smallest values. Here largest value is \(20\), smallest is \(5\), so range is \(20 - 5 = 15\). Option D gives the largest value itself, not the range.

source_trace.content_anchor: difference between the largest value and smallest value

explanations_tiered.definition: Range is the difference between the largest and smallest values in a data set.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Range measures how spread out the values are by looking only at the extremes. It is the simplest measure of dispersion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of range like the span from the shortest to the tallest person in a line: subtract the smallest from the largest.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Range is calculated as \(R = L - S\). It gives the total spread but ignores how values are distributed between the extremes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The range is sensitive to outliers because it depends only on extreme values. For \(\{5,7,9,12,15,20\}\), \(R=15\), but adding an extreme value like \(100\) would make \(R=95\).
reasoning_steps: Find the maximum number in the set.
Find the minimum number in the set.
Subtract the minimum from the maximum.
misconceptions: Confusing range with the largest value alone is incorrect because range requires subtraction of the smallest value.
Using the difference between any two middle values instead of extremes gives a different measure, not the range.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Range: \(L - S\). Quick spread, sensitive to outliers.

glossary_entry.definition: The difference between the largest and smallest values in a data set.

flashcard.front: How is the range of a data set calculated?
flashcard.back: Range = largest value \(-\) smallest value. Example: for \(\{5,7,9\}\), range = \(9 - 5 = 4\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the range of the data set \(\{5,7,9,12,15,20\}\)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Range = \(20 - 5 = 15\). It is the difference between the maximum and minimum values.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The difference between the largest and smallest values in a data set.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: students report the largest value instead of the difference. Emphasize subtraction.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate and interpret the range of ungrouped data

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For a grouped frequency distribution, the highest class has upper boundary \(24.5\) and the lowest class has lower boundary \(4.5\). What is the range?
questions.hint: Range for grouped data = upper boundary of highest class minus lower boundary of lowest class.
questions.explanation: The range for grouped data is \(R = B_U(H) - B_L(L)\). Here \(B_U(H) = 24.5\) and \(B_L(L) = 4.5\), so \(R = 24.5 - 4.5 = 20\). Option C incorrectly adds the boundaries instead of subtracting.

source_trace.content_anchor: difference between the upper class boundary of the highest class and lower class boundary of the lowest class

explanations_tiered.definition: The range for grouped data is the difference between the upper boundary of the highest class and the lower boundary of the lowest class.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Grouped data uses class boundaries instead of raw values because individual data points are not available.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine the highest class ends at 24.5 and the lowest starts at 4.5 — the span from start to end is \(24.5 - 4.5 = 20\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For grouped frequency distributions, range = \(B_U(\text{highest}) - B_L(\text{lowest})\). Boundaries ensure continuity between classes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Using boundaries avoids gaps between class intervals. If highest class is \(20-24\) and lowest is \(0-4\), \(B_U=24.5, B_L=-0.5\) gives range \(25\), not \(24-0=24\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify upper boundary of highest class: \(24.5\).
Step 2: Identify lower boundary of lowest class: \(4.5\).
reasoning_steps: Find the upper boundary of the highest class interval.
Find the lower boundary of the lowest class interval.
Subtract the lower boundary from the upper boundary.
misconceptions: Using class limits instead of boundaries gives a different (often smaller) value.
Subtracting the smallest class limit from the largest class limit ignores the gap between intervals.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Grouped range: upper boundary − lower boundary. Use boundaries, not limits.

glossary_entry.definition: The difference between the upper boundary of the highest class and the lower boundary of the lowest class.

flashcard.front: How do you calculate the range for grouped data?
flashcard.back: Range = upper boundary of highest class − lower boundary of lowest class. Example: boundaries 24.5 and 4.5 give range 20.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the range of a grouped distribution with highest class upper boundary 24.5 and lowest class lower boundary 4.5?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Range = \(24.5 - 4.5 = 20\). Use class boundaries, not raw limits.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The difference between the upper boundary of the highest class and the lower boundary of the lowest class in a grouped frequency distribution.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Grouped range: upper boundary − lower boundary.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often mistakenly use class limits. Reinforce the use of boundaries for continuity.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate the range for grouped frequency distributions

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For a data set, the first quartile \(Q_1 = 30\) and the third quartile \(Q_3 = 90\). What is the inter‑quartile range?
questions.explanation: The inter‑quartile range (IQR) is defined as \(Q_3 - Q_1\). Here \(Q_3 = 90\) and \(Q_1 = 30\), so \(IQR = 90 - 30 = 60\). Option D incorrectly adds the quartiles instead of subtracting.

source_trace.content_anchor: difference between the upper and lower quartiles

explanations_tiered.definition: Inter‑quartile range (IQR) is the difference between the third quartile and the first quartile: \(IQR = Q_3 - Q_1\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: IQR measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data, ignoring extreme values.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you line up all numbers in order, the middle half spans from Q1 to Q3. IQR is the width of that middle half.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: IQR = \(Q_3 - Q_1\). It is a robust measure of dispersion because it is not affected by outliers.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a normal distribution, \(IQR \approx 1.35\sigma\). IQR is used to identify outliers: any point below \(Q_1 - 1.5\times IQR\) or above \(Q_3 + 1.5\times IQR\) is an outlier.
reasoning_steps: Find the first quartile (25th percentile).
Find the third quartile (75th percentile).
Subtract Q1 from Q3.
misconceptions: Thinking IQR includes all data — it only covers the middle 50%.
Confusing IQR with quartile deviation: quartile deviation = \(IQR / 2\).

glossary_entry.definition: The difference between the third and first quartiles, representing the spread of the middle 50% of data.

flashcard.front: What is the inter‑quartile range (IQR) of a data set with \(Q_1 = 30\) and \(Q_3 = 90\)?
flashcard.back: \(IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 90 - 30 = 60\). It measures the spread of the middle half of the data.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is the inter‑quartile range (IQR) calculated?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The difference between the third and first quartiles; measures spread of the middle 50% of data.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: IQR = Q3 − Q1. Robust to outliers.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use box plots to visualise IQR. Common error: reporting quartile deviation instead of IQR.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate and interpret the inter‑quartile range

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Find the mean deviation about the mean for the data set \( \{10, 20, 30, 40\} \).
questions.hint: Mean deviation = average of absolute deviations from the mean.
questions.explanation: Mean \(\bar{x} = 25\). Absolute deviations: \(|10-25|=15, |20-25|=5, |30-25|=5, |40-25|=15\). Sum = \(40\), divided by 4 gives \(10\). Option A is half the correct value, a common calculation error.

source_trace.content_anchor: sum of absolute deviations divided by n

explanations_tiered.definition: Mean deviation about the mean is the average of the absolute differences between each data point and the arithmetic mean.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mean deviation measures average spread using absolute values, avoiding cancellation of positive and negative deviations.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like finding how far each number is from the average, ignoring direction, then taking the average of those distances.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: First compute mean \(\bar{x}\). Then find \(|x_i - \bar{x}|\) for each value, sum them, and divide by \(n\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mean deviation is less sensitive to outliers than variance but more sensitive than median absolute deviation. It uses \(L_1\) norm.
reasoning_steps: Calculate the mean of the data.
Subtract the mean from each value and take absolute value.
Sum all absolute deviations.
Divide the sum by the number of observations.
misconceptions: Using squared deviations instead of absolute — that gives variance, not mean deviation.
Forgetting absolute value leads to sum zero.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: MD = average of absolute deviations from mean. Units same as data.

glossary_entry.definition: The average of the absolute differences between each data point and the mean.

flashcard.front: What is the mean deviation about the mean for \(\{10,20,30,40\}\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate mean deviation about the mean for \(\{10,20,30,40\}\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Average of absolute deviations from the mean.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often forget absolute values. Emphasize that deviations must be positive.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate mean deviation about the mean for ungrouped data
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Mean = 25, sum of absolute deviations = 40, MD = 10.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Mean deviation for discrete frequency distributions
questions.question: For the discrete frequency distribution \(x: 2,4,6\) with frequencies \(f: 1,2,1\), what is the mean deviation about the mean?
questions.hint: First find the mean, then weighted absolute deviations, then divide by total frequency.
questions.explanation: Total frequency \(N=1+2+1=4\). Mean \(\bar{x} = (2\times1 + 4\times2 + 6\times1)/4 = (2+8+6)/4 = 16/4 = 4\). Absolute deviations: \(|2-4|=2\), \(|4-4|=0\), \(|6-4|=2\). Weighted sum = \(1\times2 + 2\times0 + 1\times2 = 2+0+2=4\). MD = \(4/4 = 1\). Wait — correction: my calculation gives 1, but that matches option A? Let me re‑compute carefully: Option C is 1.5. I must have made an error. Let me check: \(2,4,6\) with freqs 1,2,1. Values: 2,4,4,6. Mean = \((2+4+4+6)/4 = 16/4 = 4\). Absolute deviations: |2-4|=2, |4-4|=0, |4-4|=0, |6-4|=2. Sum = 4. MD = 4/4 = 1.0. That is option A, not C. I must correct. Regenerating this question correctly.

source_trace.structural_element: Mean deviation for discrete frequency distributions
source_trace.content_anchor: weighted sum of absolute deviations divided by total frequency

explanations_tiered.definition: Mean deviation for frequency data is the weighted average of absolute deviations, using frequencies as weights.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each deviation is multiplied by its frequency before summing, then divided by total frequency.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a value appears more often, it contributes more to the average deviation. Multiply each deviation by how many times it occurs.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Formula: \(MD = \frac{\sum f_i|x_i - \bar{x}|}{\sum f_i}\). Mean must be calculated first using weighted average.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For discrete distributions, mean deviation minimises the sum of absolute deviations about the median, not the mean. Using mean gives a slightly different value.
reasoning_steps: Compute the weighted mean.
For each value, find absolute deviation from mean.
Multiply each deviation by its frequency.
Sum weighted deviations and divide by total frequency.
misconceptions: Using unweighted sum of deviations (ignoring frequencies) underestimates spread.
Using sum of squared deviations gives variance, not mean deviation.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: For frequency data: MD = \(\frac{\sum f|x-\bar{x}|}{\sum f}\).

glossary_entry.definition: The average of absolute deviations weighted by frequencies.

flashcard.front: How do you calculate mean deviation for a frequency distribution?
flashcard.back: MD = \(\frac{\sum f|x-\bar{x}|}{\sum f}\). First find weighted mean, then weighted absolute deviations, then divide.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: For x: 2,4,6 with frequencies 1,2,1, what is MD about the mean?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Mean = 4, weighted absolute sum = 4, N=4, MD = 1.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Weighted average of absolute deviations from the mean.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often compute unweighted mean deviation. Emphasise weighting by frequency.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate mean deviation for discrete frequency distributions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A (corrected). Weighted mean = 4, sum f|x-mean| = 4, MD = 1.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If \(Q_1 = 20\) and \(Q_3 = 60\), what is the quartile deviation?
questions.explanation: Quartile deviation (QD) is half the inter‑quartile range: \(QD = \frac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2} = \frac{60 - 20}{2} = 20\). Option C gives the IQR, not QD.

source_trace.content_anchor: half of the difference between the upper and lower quartiles

explanations_tiered.definition: Quartile deviation (semi‑interquartile range) is half the difference between the third and first quartiles.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: QD measures the average spread of the middle 50% of the data, but only uses two points.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Take the middle half of your data, find its width (IQR), then split that width in half — that is QD.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: QD = \(\frac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}\). It is a measure of dispersion less affected by extremes than range.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For symmetric distributions, QD ≈ 0.675σ. QD is often used with median to describe skewed distributions.
reasoning_steps: Find the difference between the third and first quartiles.
Divide that difference by 2.
misconceptions: Confusing QD with IQR (forgetting to divide by 2).
Thinking QD uses all data — it only uses Q1 and Q3.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: QD = \((Q_3 - Q_1)/2\). Average spread of middle half.

glossary_entry.definition: Half of the inter‑quartile range; a measure of dispersion based on quartiles.

flashcard.front: What is the quartile deviation when \(Q_1 = 20\) and \(Q_3 = 60\)?
flashcard.back: QD = \((60 - 20)/2 = 20\). It is half of the inter‑quartile range.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Half the difference between the third and first quartiles.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common mistake: reporting IQR instead of QD. Emphasize division by 2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Calculate the variance of the ungrouped data set \(\{2, 4, 6, 8\}\).
questions.hint: Variance = average of squared deviations from the mean.
questions.explanation: Mean \(\bar{x} = (2+4+6+8)/4 = 5\). Squared deviations: \((2-5)^2=9, (4-5)^2=1, (6-5)^2=1, (8-5)^2=9\). Sum = 20. Variance = \(20/4 = 5\). Option B gives the mean deviation (without squaring).

source_trace.content_anchor: average of the squared difference of each value from the mean

explanations_tiered.definition: Variance is the average of the squared deviations from the mean.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Variance measures spread by squaring differences, giving more weight to larger deviations.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find how far each number is from the average, square those distances, then average the squares.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Formula: \(\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}\). Squaring eliminates negative signs and emphasises outliers.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Variance is the second central moment. It is sensitive to outliers because deviations are squared. For a sample, use \(n-1\) to get unbiased estimate.
reasoning_steps: Calculate the mean.
Subtract mean from each value and square the result.
Sum all squared deviations.
Divide by the number of values.
misconceptions: Not squaring the deviations (that gives mean deviation).
Dividing by n−1 when population variance (n) is required.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Variance = average of squared deviations from mean. Units are squared.

glossary_entry.definition: The average of squared differences between each data point and the mean.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you calculate variance for ungrouped data \(\{2,4,6,8\}\)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Average squared deviation from the mean.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often forget to square deviations. Reinforce squaring before averaging.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Mean=5, sum of squared deviations=20, variance=5.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A grouped frequency distribution has midpoints \(m_i = 5, 15, 25\) with frequencies \(f_i = 2, 3, 1\). The mean is \(15\). What is the variance?
questions.hint: Variance for grouped data = \(\frac{\sum f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2}{\sum f_i}\).
questions.explanation: Total frequency \(N = 2+3+1 = 6\). Squared deviations: \((5-15)^2=100\), \((15-15)^2=0\), \((25-15)^2=100\). Weighted sum = \(2\times100 + 3\times0 + 1\times100 = 200 + 0 + 100 = 300\). Variance = \(300/6 = 50\). Wait, that gives 50, but option C is 50, not B. I must correct: my calculation yields 50, which is option C, not B. Correct answer should be C. Let me fix:

source_trace.content_anchor: sum of f times squared deviation of midpoints from mean divided by sum f

explanations_tiered.definition: For grouped data, variance is the weighted average of squared deviations of class midpoints from the mean.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each class contributes its frequency times the squared deviation of its midpoint from the overall mean.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Treat each midpoint as representing all values in its class, then weight by how many values are in that class.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Use formula \(\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum f_i (m_i - \bar{x})^2}{\sum f_i}\). This approximates variance from grouped data.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Grouped variance is an approximation; the exact variance would use raw data. Sheppard's correction can adjust for grouping bias.
reasoning_steps: Find the mean of the grouped data.
For each class, subtract mean from midpoint and square.
Multiply each squared deviation by class frequency.
Sum weighted deviations and divide by total frequency.
misconceptions: Using class limits instead of midpoints.
Forgetting to multiply by frequency before summing.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Grouped variance: \(\frac{\sum f(m-\bar{x})^2}{\sum f}\). Use midpoints.

glossary_entry.definition: Approximate variance calculated from a frequency table using class midpoints.

flashcard.front: How do you find variance for grouped data?
flashcard.back: σ² = \(\frac{\sum f(m-\bar{x})^2}{\sum f}\), where m is class midpoint.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: For grouped data with m=5,15,25; f=2,3,1; mean=15, what is variance?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Weighted average of squared deviations of midpoints from mean.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ensure students use midpoints, not raw class limits.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C (corrected). Weighted sum of squared deviations = 300, N=6, variance=50.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If the variance of a data set is \(36\), what is the standard deviation?
questions.hint: Standard deviation is the positive square root of variance.
questions.explanation: Standard deviation \(\sigma = \sqrt{\text{variance}} = \sqrt{36} = 6\). Option D gives \(2\times\) variance, a common error.

source_trace.content_anchor: positive square root of a variance

explanations_tiered.definition: Standard deviation is the positive square root of the variance.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Standard deviation measures spread in the original units of the data, unlike variance which is in squared units.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If variance is like a squared distance, standard deviation takes the square root to get back to the original measurement unit.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}\). It is the most common measure of dispersion in statistics.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a normal distribution, about 68% of data lies within 1 standard deviation of the mean, 95% within 2σ.
reasoning_steps: Find the variance of the data set.
Take the square root of that value.
misconceptions: Forgetting to take square root — reporting variance as standard deviation.
Using absolute value instead of square root.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: σ = √variance. Same units as original data.

glossary_entry.definition: The positive square root of the variance, measuring spread in original units.

flashcard.front: If variance = 36, what is the standard deviation?
flashcard.back: σ = √36 = 6. Standard deviation is the square root of variance.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the relationship between variance and standard deviation?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Standard deviation = √variance. Example: variance 36 → standard deviation 6.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Square root of variance; measures spread in original units.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: reporting variance instead of σ. Reinforce square root.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Find the mean deviation about the median for the data set \(\{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}\).
questions.hint: Mean deviation about median = average of absolute deviations from the median.
questions.explanation: Median = middle value = 30. Absolute deviations: |10-30|=20, |20-30|=10, |30-30|=0, |40-30|=10, |50-30|=20. Sum = 60. MD = 60/5 = 12. Option A gives the mean deviation about the mean (which is also 12 in this symmetric case, but median is simpler).

source_trace.content_anchor: sum of absolute deviations from median divided by n

explanations_tiered.definition: Mean deviation about the median is the average of absolute deviations from the median.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Using median instead of mean makes the measure robust to outliers.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find the middle number (median), see how far each number is from it, then average those distances.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For odd n, median is the middle value. Compute \(|x_i - m_d|\) for each, sum, divide by n.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mean deviation about median is minimised when using median as central value; it is smaller than or equal to mean deviation about mean for any distribution.
reasoning_steps: Arrange data in ascending order and find median.
Find absolute difference between each value and median.
Average those differences.
misconceptions: Finding median incorrectly (e.g., using mean formula).
Forgetting absolute values.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: MD about median = avg(|x − median|). Robust to outliers.

glossary_entry.definition: Average absolute distance of data points from the median.

flashcard.front: Find mean deviation about median for \(\{10,20,30,40,50\}\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate mean deviation about median for 10,20,30,40,50.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Average absolute deviation from the median.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that median is the middle value, not the mean.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Median=30, sum of absolute deviations=60, MD=12.
